Client 2: Uh, net is like… maybe twelve a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes and deductions. Daniel, do you know the gross annual for the household?
Client 1: Yeah—gross household is about two hundred ten thousand a year. Like $210,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And just to sanity-check, that’s combining your income streams together, right?
Client 1: Right—between my stuff and Emil’s job.

Advisor: Great—going back to that in a minute. Before income details, I want to understand the monthly burn. About how much do you spend per month total—everything, living costs, subscriptions, food, all of it?
Client 2: Like… fifteen thousand?
Client 1: No, it’s higher. It’s around $16,650 a month total expenses.
Client 2: Okay—yeah, I was thinking just the card spend. Sorry.
Advisor: No, that’s a super common mix-up—card spend versus total outflow. So $16,650 per month total expenses. Let me jot that down—one sec.

Advisor: Within that, how much is debt payments each month—like mortgage, any loans, minimums—what’s the monthly debt cost total?
Client 2: The mortgage is… seven thousand something. That’s basically it.
Client 1: It’s $7,400 a month for the mortgage payment.
Advisor: Okay, $7,400 monthly debt cost, and that’s all mortgage—no car loans, no student loans?
Client 2: No student loans. No car loan right now.

Advisor: Perfect. Now, assets—what you’ve built up that’s investable. Roughly what do you have across investment accounts and cash that’s earmarked for investing? Total investable assets.
Client 2: We don’t have that much cash. Like maybe thirty grand?
Client 1: Investable total is about $371,900. It’s mostly in accounts, not checking.
Client 2: Wait—are you counting the house in that?
Client 1: No, not the house. Just investable stuff.

Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you, Emil. Okay, going back to what you said earlier about the house being separate from investable assets—let’s pin down the home value itself.
Advisor: Daniel, what would you say the current value of your primary residence is, roughly?
Client 1: It’s about $88,900—call it just under ninety grand.
Client 2: Wait, I thought it was like ninety-five?
Client 1: No—Emil, that was the old estimate. The one we’ve been using is $88,900.

Client 2: Yeah, I always mix those up. The $7,400 is what we pay each month, right?
Advisor: Exactly—$7,400 is the monthly cost. What I need now is the outstanding balance—how much is left on the mortgage total.
Client 1: Outstanding is about $64,900.
Advisor: $64,900 left—thank you. And just to confirm, that’s the only loan balance you have across everything?
Client 2: I mean—yeah, basically.

Client 2: Yeah, I always mix those up. The $7,400 is what we pay each month, right?
Advisor: Exactly—$7,400 is the monthly cost. What I need now is the outstanding balance—how much is left on the mortgage total.
Client 1: Outstanding is about $64,900.
Advisor: $64,900 left—thank you. And just to confirm, that’s the only loan balance you have across everything?
Client 2: I mean—yeah, basically.

Client 2: Uh, net is like… maybe twelve a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes and deductions. Daniel, do you know the gross annual for the household?
Client 1: Yeah—gross household is about two hundred ten thousand a year. Like $210,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And just to sanity-check, that’s combining your income streams together, right?
Client 1: Right—between my stuff and Emil’s job.

Client 2: Uh, net is like… maybe twelve a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes and deductions. Daniel, do you know the gross annual for the household?
Client 1: Yeah—gross household is about two hundred ten thousand a year. Like $210,050-ish.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. And just to sanity-check, that’s combining your income streams together, right?
Client 1: Right—between my stuff and Emil’s job.

Advisor: Perfect. Sorry, I know that sounds obvious, but it matters for planning assumptions.
Client 2: No, I get it.
Advisor: One more admin-ish thing, and then we’ll get back to the fun stuff—how did you find me? Like are we doing this under a US RIA relationship, or is this through something else?
Client 2: Wait—RIA like… retirement account?
Advisor: Easy mix-up. RIA as in “registered investment advisor,” the advisory firm structure—not an IRA retirement account.

Advisor: Thanks—that helps. We can come back if needed.
Client 1: Sure.
Advisor: Cool. Going back to what you said earlier about wanting guardrails—Daniel, Emil—how would you describe your overall risk comfort? Like conservative, moderate, aggressive?
Client 1: I’d call us moderate. I’m not trying to swing for the fences, but I do want growth.
Client 2: I’m more cautious than Daniel, but yeah—overall moderate is fair.

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emil—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going?
Client 2: Pretty good—busy. Traffic was kind of a mess.
Client 1: Yeah, same. We’re good though.
Advisor: Got it. So, tell me a little about what brought you in, Daniel—what are you hoping to get out of today?

Client 1: Busy, but good. Okay—yeah.
Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I get that. Daniel, Emil, going back to what you said earlier about not having a ton of cushion, I want to make sure I’ve got the household basics right before we talk next steps.
Advisor: First one’s simple: are you two legally married, or is it more like domestic partners?
Client 2: Married—like actually married.
Client 1: Yeah, married.

Client 2: Is that like… the percent that comes out of my paycheck? Because mine feels like, I don’t know, 20-something?
Advisor: Good question—so that’s more like withholding and effective rate. I mean the marginal bracket—the top slice of income. Daniel, do you remember what you told me last time?
Client 1: Yeah, we’re in the 35% bracket federally.
Client 2: Wait, 35? That sounds too high.
Client 1: It’s the marginal one, Emil. Not what hits every dollar.

Client 1: Mostly a plan that’s not too complicated. We make good money but it still feels… tight month to month sometimes.
Client 2: And I want to feel like we’re not one surprise expense away from freaking out.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Let’s start with the big picture numbers and then we’ll circle back to goals. Roughly, what’s your total household gross income per year—before taxes?
Client 2: Uh, net is like… maybe twelve a month?
Advisor: Okay—so that’s take-home. I’m asking gross, before taxes and deductions. Daniel, do you know the gross annual for the household?

Advisor: Hi Daniel, hi Emil—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how’s your day going?
Client 2: Pretty good—busy. Traffic was kind of a mess.
Client 1: Yeah, same. We’re good though.
Advisor: Got it. So, tell me a little about what brought you in, Daniel—what are you hoping to get out of today?

Advisor: Yep—totally fair. I mean gross annual, so the salary number on your offer letter or W-2, roughly.
Client 2: Oh—gross. Sorry. It’s about sixty-five, six hundred a year.
Client 1: Yeah, $65,600 for Emil.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And Emil, what do you do—what’s your occupation area? Like operations, sales, engineering—what bucket would you put it in?
Client 2: Operations. Like ops—process stuff, coordinating.

Advisor: Totally. Income first: Daniel, you’ve got two main income streams you mentioned—one is rental income monthly, and the other is business income that comes in quarterly. Still true?
Client 1: Yes—rental monthly, business quarterly.
Advisor: And Emil, you’re employed as well—W-2 type paycheck, right?
Client 2: Yeah, I’m employed.
Advisor: Great. Assets—Daniel, you had that big taxable account on a retirement platform—sorry, I know that sounds weird—taxable but held at a platform you associate with retirement tools. Still the main bucket?

Advisor: Great. And I’m going to ask a slightly annoying admin question—birthdates—just month, day, year.
Advisor: Emil, what’s your date of birth?
Client 2: Uh, November 4th, 1999.
Advisor: November 4, 1999—thank you.
Advisor: And Daniel, your date of birth?

Advisor: Busy but good—yeah, I hear you. Okay, going back to that income picture you laid out earlier, Daniel, I want to make sure I’ve got your base pay right too.
Advisor: Daniel, what’s your gross annual income from your job—ballpark is fine, then we’ll tighten it up?
Client 1: Roughly like $145k a year.
Client 2: I thought it was closer to $140k?
Client 1: No—Emil, that was last year. It’s $144,400 gross.

Advisor: November 4, 1999—thank you.
Advisor: And Daniel, your date of birth?
Client 1: January 12th, 1998.
Client 2: Wait—are you sure it’s the 12th? I always think it’s the 21st.
Client 1: No, it’s the 12th. The 21st is my brother—different person.

Advisor: Okay. And just so I’m not missing anything—your investable assets include your taxable account, Emil’s retirement account, and the joint bank account you mentioned earlier?
Client 1: Yeah—my taxable is the big one, Emil has the 401k/IRA bucket, and we’ve got a joint bank account.
Client 2: And the house is separate—primary residence.
Advisor: Right. We’ll map those out in detail next. Quick tangent—are you both working full-time right now?
Client 1: Yes.

Client 2: Yep—Daniel’s Client 1.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. One sec, I’m just writing that down.
